title: (GL_k × Sym_n)-modules and Nabla of hook-indexed Schur functions
overall_summary: The paper constructs, for every hook partition rho=(a|b) of n in Frobenius notation, explicit multigraded GL_infinity × S_n modules M_rho and quotient modules S_rho generated from a punctured Vandermonde-type determinant by derivations and higher polarization operators. Their graded Frobenius characteristics have tensor Schur expansions S_rho^⊗ = sum c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, so the paper works directly in the stable GL-character framework in which universal multigraded Hilbert coefficients naturally live. The main hook-specific claim is conjectural: after restricting the GL side to Schur functions of length at most two, S_rho^⊗ should equal ∇ applied to a normalized hook-indexed Schur function; the paper proves supporting one-set/t=0 specializations and skew-restriction identities. The paper also records a shuffle/LLT formula for ∇ of normalized hook Schur functions, one-set Hall-Littlewood/Kostka descriptions, conjectural hook-component formulas via skewing by e_a^⊥, and plethystic e-positivity phenomena. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most relevant content is the stable Schur-multiplicity setup, the Dyck path/LLT formula for hook-indexed nabla expressions, and the paper's methods for isolating hook components through quotient filtrations and skewing operators.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 construction: determinant-generated hook modules and their Frobenius expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n≥1 and a hook rho=(a|b), meaning the partition (a+1,1^b) with a,b≥0 and a+b=n−1. Let X be an infinite matrix of variables with n columns; its rows are sets u=(u_1,...,u_n), with first row x=(x_1,...,x_n). Let theta_1,...,theta_n be inert variables of degree 0. Define D_(a|b)(x) as the n by n determinant whose i-th row is (theta_i, 1, x_i, ..., x_i^{a-1}, x_i^{a+1}, ..., x_i^{n-1}), i.e. the Vandermonde columns 1,x,...,x^{n-1} with the x^a column removed and an inert theta column inserted. For rows u,v of X and integer r≥1 define the higher polarization operator E_uv^(r)=sum_{i=1}^n v_i ∂^r/∂u_i^r. The module M_(a|b) is the smallest subspace of Q[X,theta] containing D_(a|b)(x) and closed under the allowed derivations and all higher polarizations; equivalently it is written in the paper as Q({∂_{x_i}}; {E_uv^(r)}) D_(a|b)(x). The total derivative satisfies sum_i ∂_{x_i} D_(a|b)(x) = (a+1) D_(a+1|b−1)(x) for 0≤a<n−1, and is 0 otherwise; hence the M_(a|b) form a filtration M_(n−1|0) ⊂ M_(n−2|1) ⊂ ... ⊂ M_(0|n−1). With the convention M_(n|−1)=0, define the quotient S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1). The N^infinity-graded Frobenius characteristic has a Schur-tensor expansion S_rho^⊗ = sum_{mu⊢n} sum_lambda c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, where s_lambda is the GL_infinity character in the grading variables and s_mu is the Frobenius characteristic of the S_n-isotypic component. The coefficient of s_mu on the S_n side is denoted c_{rho,mu}=sum_lambda c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda, and the alternant coefficient is A_rho=c_{rho,1^n}. The length restriction operator L_{≤k} keeps only those terms with l(lambda)≤k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the basic construction rather than a theorem. The determinant D_(a|b) is homogeneous in the row grading except for the inert theta column, and the actions F(X)↦F(gX sigma) of GL_infinity and S_n commute. The displayed total-derivative identity is obtained by differentiating the determinant with respect to all x_i and using the fact that differentiating the sequence of powers shifts the missing column; all terms cancel or combine to the determinant with the next omitted power, up to the scalar a+1. Since M_(a|b) is closed under these derivations, D_(a+1|b−1) lies in M_(a|b), giving the filtration. Semisimplicity of polynomial GL_infinity representations and of S_n representations gives the Schur-tensor expansion with nonnegative multiplicities for the quotient module. Restricting to finitely many rows, i.e. k sets of variables, corresponds to retaining only GL Schur functions of length at most k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1, shuffle formula for ∇ of normalized hook Schur functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let rho=(a|b) be a hook of size n=a+b+1. The paper uses the normalization s_hat_rho = (-1/(q t))^{iota_rho} s_rho, where iota_(a|b)=a. Let delta(n)=(n−1,n−2,...,1,0), and identify a Dyck path gamma below the diagonal with a partition gamma contained in delta(n). Define Gamma_a = delta(n) − (0,...,0,1,...,1,0), where there are a consecutive copies of 1 as in the paper. For each Dyck path gamma⊆Gamma_a, let area(gamma) be its usual area and let L_gamma(q;z) be the associated vertical-strip LLT polynomial, defined as a dinv-weighted generating function over semistandard Young tableaux of skew shape (gamma+1^n)/gamma. Then ∇(s_hat_(a|b))(q,t;z) = sum_{gamma⊆Gamma_a} t^{area(gamma)−a} L_gamma(q;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove the shuffle formula from first principles; it states that the identity follows from results of Haglund-Morse-Zabrocki together with the compositional shuffle theorems of Carlsson-Mellit and Mellit. The compositional shuffle framework expresses ∇ applied to the relevant hook-indexed Schur input as a sum over Dyck paths with prescribed constraints; for rho=(a|b), those constraints are encoded by containment gamma⊆Gamma_a. For each fixed path gamma, summing over the semistandard fillings of the vertical-strip skew shape (gamma+1^n)/gamma with dinv weight gives precisely the LLT polynomial L_gamma(q;z). The factor t^{area(gamma)−a} records the shifted area contribution induced by the hook normalization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.2, module conjecture for hook-indexed nabla
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a hook rho=(a|b), expand ∇(s_hat_rho) in tensor notation as ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ = sum_{mu⊢n} sum_lambda b^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, meaning that ∇(s_hat_rho)(q,t;z)=sum b^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda(q,t) s_mu(z). The conjecture states that, for every hook rho, L_{≤2}(S_rho^⊗)=∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗. Equivalently, for every rho, every mu⊢n, and every partition lambda of length at most 2, the multiplicity c^rho_{lambda,mu} in S_rho equals the coefficient b^rho_{lambda,mu} in ∇(s_hat_rho).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a conjecture, not proved in the paper. The paper provides several forms of evidence: explicit computations for all hooks of sizes n≤4, with the first discrepancy between S_rho^⊗ and ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ occurring only in GL length 3; a proof of the t=0 or one-set specialization in Proposition 1.6; and skew-operator consistency results in Theorem 1.4. The conjecture is deliberately formulated with L_{≤2} because ∇(s_hat_rho)(q,t;z) is naturally a two-grading object, while S_rho exists with arbitrarily many sets of variables.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.3 and Theorem 1.4, skew identities and their length-two verification
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let e_1^⊥ denote the operator adjoint to multiplication by e_1 under the Hall inner product. Conjecture 1.3 asserts two skew identities for the quotient modules: (Id ⊗ e_1^⊥) S_(n)^⊗ = sum_{a=0}^{n−2} S_(a|n−a−2)^⊗, where S_(n) means S_(n−1|0); and (e_1^⊥ ⊗ Id) S_{1^n}^⊗ = sum_{a=1}^{n−1} S_(a|n−a−1)^⊗, where S_{1^n} means S_(0|n−1). The paper states that these identities were checked computationally for n≤6. Theorem 1.4 proves the length-two restriction of the first identity. It also states, under the paper's standing use of the cited conjectural input from Bergeron's earlier work and assuming Conjecture 1.2, that the length-two restriction of the second identity holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first identity, L_{≤2} commutes with Id ⊗ e_1^⊥ because length restriction concerns the GL side, while e_1^⊥ acts on the S_n Frobenius side. Representation-theoretically, Id ⊗ e_1^⊥ is restriction of the S_n action to the subgroup S_{n−1} fixing one letter. The paper invokes the lattice-diagram discussion of Bergeron-Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler to identify (Id ⊗ e_1^⊥) L_{≤2}(S_(n)^⊗) with the derivation-polarization span of a determinant D^− obtained by omitting the variable x_n; in the notation of the paper this span is isomorphic to M_{(1|n−2)}^{<2>}. Unfolding the definition S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1) gives the sum of the length-two restricted quotients. For the second identity, after applying Conjecture 1.2, the desired statement becomes an identity involving e_1^⊥∇ applied to normalized hook Schur functions. The proof uses an operator identity from Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler, e_1^⊥ ∇ = ∇(e_1^⊥ Δ_{e_1} − Δ_{e_1} e_1^⊥), and the identity Δ_{e_1} s_hat_n = sum_{a=0}^{n−1} s_hat_(a|n−a−1), cited from Bergeron-Garsia-Sergel Leven-Xin. The final comparison uses the cited conjectural identification from Bergeron's earlier paper relating Δ'_{e_{n−2}} e_n to the relevant length-two part of (e_1^⊥ ⊗ Id) S_{1^n}^⊗.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5, Haiman n!-Theorem as used in the paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Ferrers diagram mu with n cells, let D_mu(x,y)=det(x_i^k y_i^l) be the lattice diagram determinant, with cells (k,l) ordered as in the paper, and let G_mu be the module obtained by closing D_mu under all partial derivatives in the x and y variables. Haiman's n!-Theorem states that the bigraded Frobenius characteristic of G_mu is the combinatorial Macdonald polynomial H_tilde_mu(q,t;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is quoted from Haiman and not proved in the paper. The paper uses it as an input to identify certain one-set pieces of the hook modules M_(a|b) with top y-degree components of Garsia-Haiman modules. In particular, when the relevant lattice diagram is the Ferrers hook (n−2|1), the coefficient of t in H_tilde_(n−2|1)(q,t;z) gives the one-set Frobenius characteristic needed for M_(n−1|0).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (25), one-set Hall-Littlewood formula for M_(a|b) and S_(a|b)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H_mu(q;z) be the symmetric function H_mu=omega Q'_{mu'} associated with the dual Hall-Littlewood function Q'_{mu'}, so that H_mu(q;z)=sum_lambda K_{lambda',mu'}(q) s_lambda(z), with K_{lambda,mu}(q) the Kostka-Foulkes polynomials. For every hook rho=(a|b) of size n, so a+b=n−1, the one-set graded Frobenius characteristic of M_(a|b) is M_(a|b)(q;z) = (1+q+...+q^b) H_(n−2|1)(q;z). Consequently, since S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1), one obtains S_(a|b)(q;z)=q^b H_(n−2|1)(q;z) in one set of variables.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The determinant D_(a|b)(x) is obtained from the lattice diagram d(a,b)={(i,0): 0≤i≤a+b and i≠a} ∪ {(0,1)} by replacing the y variables by inert theta variables. Thus M_(a|b) with one row of variables is the top y-degree piece of the corresponding Garsia-Haiman-type module. For a=n−1,b=0 the diagram is the Ferrers hook (n−2|1), so Haiman's theorem identifies M_(n−1|0)(q;z) with the coefficient of t in H_tilde_(n−2|1)(q,t;z), denoted H_(n−2|1)(q;z). The total derivative pi=sum_i ∂_{x_i} maps D_(a|b) to a nonzero scalar multiple of D_(a+1|b−1), inducing a projection M_(a|b) onto M_(a+1|b−1) and a degree-one reducing isomorphism S_(a|b)≅S_(a+1|b−1). Therefore S_(a|b)(q;z)=q S_(a+1|b−1)(q;z), and telescoping down the filtration gives the displayed geometric sum for M_(a|b).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.6, t=0 specialization of the module conjecture
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every hook rho=(a|b), Conjecture 1.2 holds after setting the second nabla grading variable t equal to 0. Equivalently, the one-set specialization of S_rho matches ∇(s_hat_rho)(q,0;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper combines the one-set formula M_(a|b)(q;z)=(1+q+...+q^b) H_(n−2|1)(q;z), the induced quotient formula S_(a|b)(q;z)=q^b H_(n−2|1)(q;z), and the Hall-Littlewood/Delta identities cited from Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono. On the nabla side, Proposition 1.1 gives ∇(s_hat_(a|b))(q,t;z)=sum_{gamma⊆Gamma_a} t^{area(gamma)−a} L_gamma(q;z). When t=0, only the unique path gamma=Gamma_a with area(gamma)=a contributes, so ∇(s_hat_(a|b))(q,0;z)=L_{Gamma_a}(q;z). The cited t=0 Hall-Littlewood identities identify this LLT polynomial with the one-set Frobenius obtained from the module filtration, yielding the stated specialization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1.3 conditional Garsia-Haiman intersection formulas
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assuming conjectures from Bergeron-Garsia on intersections of Garsia-Haiman modules, let I_n = G_{d(n−2,1)} ∩ G_{d(n−1,0)}, where d(a,b) is the lattice diagram {(i,0):0≤i≤a+b and i≠a} ∪ {(0,1)} and G_d is the derivative closure of the corresponding lattice diagram determinant. Then the paper records the conditional Frobenius formula I_n(q,t;z) = (q^{n−1} H_tilde_(n−1,1)(q,t;z) − t H_tilde_(n)(q,t;z))/(q^{n−1}−t). Under the same assumptions, G_{d(n−2,1)} decomposes as I_n ⊕ I_n^⊥ with dim I_n=dim I_n^⊥=n!/2, and I_n^⊥(q,t;z)=t H_(n−1,1)(q;z). Consequently H_tilde_(n−1,1)(q,t;z)=q^{binom(n−1,2)} omega H_(n−1,1)(1/q;z)+t H_(n−1,1)(q;z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The intersection formula is not proved in this paper; it is presented as a specialization of conjectures from Bergeron-Garsia. The displayed expression for I_n^⊥ follows by subtracting the conjectural expression for I_n from H_tilde_(n−1,1), the Frobenius of G_{d(n−2,1)}, and simplifying. The final two-term formula for H_tilde_(n−1,1) separates the q-reversed omega image of the one-set Hall-Littlewood piece from the top t-degree Hall-Littlewood piece. This decomposition is then used to motivate explicit bases for one-set hook quotient modules.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.7, basis for one-set hook quotients from an intersection basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B_n be a basis of the intersection module I_n described in Section 1.3. For each a≥1 with b=n−a−1 and d=d(a,b), the set { phi(∂_x) D_d(x) : phi(x) in B_n } forms a basis of the one-set quotient S_(a|b)^{<1>}. Here D_d(x) is the determinant corresponding to the diagram d(a,b), with y variables replaced by inert theta variables, and phi(∂_x) means the differential operator obtained from phi by substituting ∂_{x_i} for x_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says it only needs to check linear independence. If phi is a nonzero element in the span of B_n, then by hypothesis phi(x)=psi(∂_x) D_d(x) for some polynomial psi. Suppose one applies psi(∂_x) to phi(∂_x)D_d(x). Since the differential operators commute, psi(∂_x) phi(∂_x)D_d(x)=phi(∂_x) psi(∂_x)D_d(x)=phi(∂_x)phi(x). The constant term of phi(∂_x)phi(x) is the sum of the squares of the coefficients of phi, hence is nonzero. Therefore phi(∂_x)D_d(x) is nonzero for every nonzero phi in the span of B_n, giving injectivity of the map phi↦phi(∂_x)D_d(x). Together with the known dimension of the quotient, this gives a basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.8, hook components from the alternant component
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every hook rho of size n and every hook mu=(a|b) of size n, with 0≤a≤n−1 and b=n−a−1, the paper conjectures c_{rho,(a|b)} = e_a^⊥ A_rho. Here c_{rho,mu}=sum_lambda c^rho_{lambda,mu} s_lambda is the GL-character coefficient of the S_n-isotypic component indexed by mu in S_rho^⊗, A_rho=c_{rho,1^n} is the alternant coefficient, and e_a^⊥ is adjoint to multiplication by e_a. The paper notes the consequence A_(n+1)=A_{1^n} from the module definition, which would imply c_{(n|0),(a|b+1)} = c_{(0|n−1),(a|b)} for all a.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a conjecture, not proved in the paper. Its content is that all hook-indexed S_n-isotypic coefficients of S_rho are obtained by skewing the single alternant coefficient A_rho. The stated consequence follows formally by applying the conjectural identity to the equality of alternant components A_(n+1)=A_{1^n}; the indices shift because the row hook (n|0) has size n+1 while 1^n has size n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 1.9 and Section 1.6, coefficient-length bound and reconstruction of alternants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a symmetric function f=sum_lambda a_lambda s_lambda, let ell(f)=max{ell(lambda): a_lambda≠0}. Conjecture 1.9 states that if rho=(a|b) is a hook of size n with a≥1, then ell(c_{rho,mu}) ≤ n−mu_1 for every partition mu⊢n. The paper combines this conjecture with the Hook Components Conjecture to describe a reconstruction of A_rho. In particular, e_{n−1}^⊥ A_rho=0, e_{n−2}^⊥ A_rho=c_{rho,(n−2|1)}=s_b, so A_rho has top-length term s_(b|n−3) plus lower-length terms. More generally, the terms of A_rho of length n−3 are determined from e_{n−3}^⊥ A_rho = c_{rho,(n−3|2)} = <S_rho^⊗, s_(n−3|2)>; by the length bound, this coefficient has length at most 2 and is therefore determined by the length-two nabla expression predicted by Conjecture 1.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is conditional reasoning, not a proved theorem. The Hook Components Conjecture turns the desired hook coefficients into successive skewings e_a^⊥ A_rho. The length conjecture says that for hook mu=(n−r|r−1), c_{rho,mu} has GL-Schur length at most r, so low r coefficients are completely visible in the length-two data once r≤2 and partially determine higher-length layers of A_rho by inversion of e_a^⊥. The paper illustrates the method for hooks of size 6: it computes the required coefficients of ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ against s_411 and then adds exactly the missing length-3 and higher Schur terms to A_rho, with the length≤2 remainder supplied by <∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗, e_6>.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1, e_n coefficient after plethystic specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define T_rho = S_rho^⊗[1+q;z] by plethystically substituting the GL alphabet q by 1+q in each coefficient c_{rho,mu}; write T_rho=sum_{nu⊢n} d_{rho,nu} ⊗ e_nu(z) in the elementary basis on the S_n side. If the Hook Components Conjecture 1.8 holds, then the coefficient d_{rho,(n)} of e_n in T_rho is Schur-positive; in fact d_{rho,(n)}=A_rho.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let f_n be the forgotten symmetric function dual to e_n. The coefficient of e_n in T_rho is <S_rho^⊗[1+q;z], f_n>. The paper uses the known pairing <s_mu,f_n>, which is 0 unless mu is a hook (k|j), and equals (−1)^k for mu=(k|j). Therefore d_{rho,(n)} = (sum_{k=0}^{n−1} (−1)^k c_{rho,(k|n−k−1)})[1+q]. Assuming Conjecture 1.8, c_{rho,(k|n−k−1)}=e_k^⊥ A_rho, so the sum becomes (sum_k (−1)^k e_k^⊥ A_rho)[1+q]. The standard plethystic identity sum_k (−1)^k e_k^⊥ F(q)=F[q−1] gives d_{rho,(n)}=(A_rho[q−1])[1+q]=A_rho. Since A_rho is an actual GL-character of a module component, it is Schur-positive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 2.2, trivariate shuffle formula for selected hooks
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For hooks rho=(a|b) with a equal to 0, 1, or n−1, the paper conjectures a trivariate formula S_rho(q,t,1;z) = sum_{Gamma_a ≤ alpha ≤ beta} q^{d(alpha,beta)} L_beta(t;z). The sum is over pairs of Dyck paths alpha,beta with alpha below beta in the Tamari poset and alpha at least Gamma_a in the indicated order. The statistic d(alpha,beta) is the length of the longest strict chain from alpha to beta in the Tamari poset, and L_beta(t;z) is the LLT polynomial associated with beta.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is conjectural in the paper. The author notes that the case a=0 is the trivariate shuffle conjecture from Bergeron-Préville-Ratelle, and that the case a=n−1 is more or less implicit in work of Bousquet-Mélou-Chapuy-Préville-Ratelle on m-Tamari intervals. The conjecture extends those Tamari-interval sums to the hook modules S_rho in three sets of variables with the third grading specialized to 1.

proof_techniques.name: Stable GL_infinity × S_n Frobenius bookkeeping
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats a multigraded module with arbitrarily many sets of variables as a polynomial GL_infinity representation commuting with S_n. A homogeneous basis of each S_n-isotypic component produces a GL-character, and polynomial GL_infinity characters expand in Schur functions s_lambda(q). This yields tensor notation sum c_{lambda,mu} s_lambda ⊗ s_mu, with the left factor recording multidegrees and the right factor recording the S_n representation. Passing to k sets of variables is implemented by the operator L_{≤k}, which discards all GL Schur functions of length greater than k.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The universal coefficients c_lambda(n) in a Schur expansion of a multigraded Hilbert series can often be interpreted as stable GL_m multiplicities; the paper's framework suggests proving hook cases by first constructing or identifying a stable GL_infinity module and then extracting the hook-shaped left Schur coefficient.

proof_techniques.name: Punctured Vandermonde determinant with inert variables
proof_techniques.description: For each hook (a|b), the determinant D_(a|b) is obtained from the Vandermonde determinant by removing the x^a column and inserting an inert theta column. Closing this determinant under derivations and higher polarization operators generates a GL_infinity × S_n module M_(a|b). The total derivative shifts the missing column from a to a+1, producing a nested filtration by hook parameters and allowing the quotient S_(a|b) to isolate the layer associated with that hook.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the coinvariant algebra or its harmonic model admits determinant generators or lattice-diagram pieces, a similar punctured-determinant filtration could isolate hook-shaped Schur contributions rather than requiring a global basis of the full algebra.

proof_techniques.name: Filtration quotient telescoping
proof_techniques.description: The identity sum_i ∂_{x_i}D_(a|b) proportional to D_(a+1|b−1) gives inclusions M_(a+1|b−1)⊂M_(a|b). The quotient definition S_(a|b)=M_(a|b)/M_(a+1|b−1) converts information about adjacent M-modules into information about the individual hook layer. In one set of variables, the induced quotient isomorphisms shift degree by one, giving S_(a|b)(q;z)=q S_(a+1|b−1)(q;z) and hence a geometric-sum formula for M_(a|b).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook coefficients in a Hilbert series might be accessible by setting up a filtration whose successive quotients correspond to hooks; telescoping can turn a hard coefficient extraction into a degree shift from a single base case.

proof_techniques.name: Dyck path and LLT polynomial expansion for hook-indexed nabla
proof_techniques.description: Using the compositional shuffle theorem, the paper expresses ∇(s_hat_(a|b)) as a sum over Dyck paths gamma contained in a hook-dependent boundary Gamma_a. Each path contributes a vertical-strip LLT polynomial L_gamma(q;z), itself a generating function over semistandard tableaux of a skew shape immediately to the right of the path, weighted by dinv, and the whole term receives an area weight t^{area(gamma)−a}. At t=0 only the extremal path Gamma_a contributes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a ready-made combinatorial model, Dyck paths plus semistandard tableaux, for hook-indexed Schur phenomena. If the desired hook c_lambda(n) can be related to a nabla hook expression or to the length-two part of S_rho, its coefficients may be interpreted by extracting Schur coefficients from these LLT sums.

proof_techniques.name: Top y-degree reduction to Hall-Littlewood and Kostka-Foulkes data
proof_techniques.description: The paper observes that replacing y variables by inert theta variables makes M_(a|b) with one set of variables the top y-degree piece of a lattice-diagram/Garsia-Haiman module. For the base hook, Haiman's n!-Theorem identifies this top piece with H_(n−2|1)=omega Q'_(n−2|1)' and hence with a Kostka-Foulkes expansion. The entire one-set family then follows from the derivative-induced degree shifts.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One-variable specializations of multigraded Hilbert coefficients often have Hall-Littlewood or Kostka-Foulkes interpretations; for hook shapes, this suggests looking for charge/Kostka counts as the t=0 shadow of the desired combinatorics.

proof_techniques.name: Skewing operators as restriction and hook extraction
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses e_1^⊥ on the S_n side as the Frobenius-characteristic operation corresponding to restricting an S_n representation to S_{n−1}. It also conjectures that e_a^⊥ on the GL side extracts hook S_n-isotypic coefficients from the alternant coefficient A_rho. These skewing operators connect branching rules, quotient filtrations, and hook components in a compact symmetric-function language.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook-shaped Schur coefficients, try expressing the coefficient as a skewing of a better-understood component, especially an alternant or sign-isotypic component. This can reduce the hook case to proving an e_a^⊥ identity rather than constructing all multigraded pieces.

proof_techniques.name: Length bounds to reconstruct Schur-positive characters
proof_techniques.description: The coefficient-length conjecture says that the GL-Schur length of c_{rho,mu} is controlled by n−mu_1. Combined with hook-component skewing, the paper reconstructs the high-length part of A_rho layer by layer from low-length coefficients that are visible in the conjectural nabla formula. This is a triangular inversion strategy based on Schur length.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook c_lambda(n) are only partially visible in low numbers of variable sets, a length bound could justify reconstruction of the full stable coefficient from two-variable or one-variable data.

proof_techniques.name: Plethystic e-positivity via forgotten symmetric functions
proof_techniques.description: The paper studies T_rho=S_rho^⊗[1+q;z], expands it in the elementary basis e_nu on the S_n side, and relates the e_n coefficient to hook Schur components by pairing with the forgotten function f_n. Because <s_mu,f_n> vanishes except for hook mu, where it is an alternating sign, the Hook Components Conjecture converts this coefficient into a plethystic difference A_rho[q−1] evaluated at 1+q, i.e. A_rho itself.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Alternating sums over hook components can sometimes be packaged as an elementary-basis coefficient after a 1+q plethystic substitution. This may provide both positivity and a combinatorial interpretation if an e-positive expansion is available.

proof_techniques.name: Differential-operator basis from module intersections
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.7 uses a basis B_n of an intersection of Garsia-Haiman modules to build a basis of one-set hook quotients by applying each polynomial phi in B_n as a differential operator phi(∂_x) to the determinant D_d. Injectivity follows from the nonzero constant term of phi(∂)phi, which is the sum of squares of coefficients. This is a standard apolarity-type argument adapted to these determinant modules.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A direct combinatorial basis for hook pieces of a coinvariant algebra might be built by applying differential operators indexed by a known basis of an intersected or simpler harmonic module, with nonvanishing certified by the same apolar pairing.

other_useful_info: Normalization and bounds: for a hook rho=(a|b), the paper uses s_hat_rho=(-1/(q t))^a s_rho. In the Schur-tensor expansion of S_rho, the GL partition lambda has at most n−1 parts and size bounded by (n choose 2)+b. Explicit computational data: the paper lists complete S_rho^⊗ expansions for all hooks of size up to 4 in the main text, hooks of size 5 and 6 in the appendix, and e-expansions of T_rho for hooks of size at most 4. The smallest case where S_rho^⊗ is larger than ∇(s_hat_rho)^⊗ is rho=1111, where S_1111^⊗ = ∇(s_hat_1111)^⊗ + s_111 ⊗ s_1111; thus the extra part has GL length 3 and is invisible to L_{≤2}. For hooks of size 5 the appendix records, for example, S_5^⊗ = ∇(s_hat_5)^⊗ + s_111 ⊗ s_11111, S_41^⊗ = ∇(s_hat_41)^⊗ + s_111 ⊗ s_2111 + s_211 ⊗ s_11111, and analogous triangular extra terms for S_311, S_2111, and S_11111. Section 2 notes that experiments suggest all coefficients d_{rho,nu} in T_rho=sum d_{rho,nu}⊗e_nu are Schur-positive; moreover, if mu is largest in dominance order among partitions with c_{rho,mu} nonzero, then d_{rho,mu'}=c_{rho,mu}[1+q]. The paper also reports the experimental hook formula d_{rho,mu}=sum_{i=0}^{min(j,k)} c_{(a|b−i),(k−i|j)} for rho=(a|b) and mu=(k|j), except in the special case rho=mu=1^n where d_{rho,mu}=1. Another observed lift of a known nabla identity is <S_(n+1)^⊗, s_(a|b)> = <S_{1^n}^⊗, s_(a|b−1)> for all a+b=n. Important cited sources for extending these ideas are: Carlsson-Mellit and Mellit for the shuffle theorem; Haglund-Rhoades-Shimozono for Hall-Littlewood/Delta identities at t=0; Bergeron's earlier GL_k × S_n diagonal-harmonics paper for the length-two conjectural input; Bergeron-Préville-Ratelle and Bousquet-Mélou-Chapuy-Préville-Ratelle for Tamari-interval/trivariate shuffle interpretations; and Bergeron-Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler for lattice diagram modules and restriction arguments.
why_relevant: Focuses specifically on GL_k x S_n structures and nabla applied to hook-indexed Schur functions. Its hook-specific multiplicity formulas and conjectures are directly aligned with the hook-shape case of c_lambda(n).
